rule.name: AddEmptyString
rule.message: Avoid using empty string concatenation to convert to String; use a string template instead
rule.description: Avoid concatenating an empty string `""` to convert a non-String value to a String. This creates an unnecessary intermediate `String` object. In Kotlin, prefer string templates (`"$value"`) or `value.toString()` for explicit conversions.

rule.name: AppendCharacterWithChar
rule.message: Use a Char literal instead of a single-character String when calling StringBuilder.append().
rule.description:
Passing a single-character string literal to StringBuilder.append(String) forces the JVM to perform a string comparison and then extract the character internally. Using the Char overload -- append('x') -- is more direct and avoids allocating a temporary String object.
Kotlin equivalent of Java rule: category/java/performance.xml/AppendCharacterWithChar

property.name: javaEquivalentRule
property.description: Java rule this Kotlin rule is based on; update this rule when the Java rule changes.

property.name: xpath

rule.name: AvoidArrayLoops
rule.message: Use copyInto() or copyOf() instead of manually copying array elements in a loop.
rule.description:
Manually copying array elements one by one in a loop is less efficient and less clear than using the built-in `copyInto()` or `copyOf()` functions. The built-in functions are implemented natively and avoid the overhead of interpreted looping.
Prefer: - `src.copyInto(dst)` for a full copy into an existing array - `src.copyOf()` or `src.copyOf(n)` to produce a new copy - `src.copyOfRange(from, to)` for a partial copy
Kotlin equivalent of Java rule: category/java/performance.xml/AvoidArrayLoops

property.name: javaEquivalentRule
property.description: Java rule this Kotlin rule is based on; update this rule when the Java rule changes.

property.name: xpath

rule.name: AvoidCalendarDateCreation
rule.message: Avoid creating Calendar instances; use the java.time API (LocalDateTime, ZonedDateTime) instead.
rule.description:
java.util.Calendar is a heavyweight, mutable object. Creating a Calendar instance just to obtain the current date or time is expensive and unnecessarily verbose.
Prefer the modern java.time API introduced in Java 8: - java.time.LocalDateTime.now() / ZonedDateTime.now() for date-time values - java.time.Instant.now() for a machine timestamp - java.time.LocalDate.today() for date-only values
Kotlin equivalent of Java rule: category/java/performance.xml/AvoidCalendarDateCreation

property.name: javaEquivalentRule
property.description: Java rule this Kotlin rule is based on; update this rule when the Java rule changes.

rule.name: AvoidCallingGcExplicitly
rule.message: Avoid explicit garbage-collection calls; the JVM manages memory automatically.
rule.description:
Calling System.gc() or Runtime.getRuntime().gc() requests a GC cycle but does not guarantee one will run. In practice it introduces unpredictable pauses and can degrade throughput. The JVM's garbage collector is designed to manage memory automatically; manual invocations are almost never correct.
Kotlin equivalent of Java rule: category/java/performance.xml/AvoidCallingGcExplicitly

property.name: javaEquivalentRule
property.description: Java rule this Kotlin rule is based on; update this rule when the Java rule changes.

rule.name: AvoidFileStream
rule.description:
FileInputStream/OutputStream/Reader/Writer contain a finalizer that can cause GC pressure. In Kotlin prefer: - File.inputStream() / File.outputStream() for byte streams - File.bufferedReader() / File.bufferedWriter() for character streams - File.readBytes() / File.readText() / File.writeText() for one-shot reads/writes - Files.newInputStream(path) / Files.newOutputStream(path) for the NIO2 approach All of these avoid the finalizer and work naturally with use {}.
Kotlin equivalent of Java rule: category/java/performance.xml/AvoidFileStream

property.name: javaEquivalentRule
property.description: Java rule this Kotlin rule is based on; update this rule when the Java rule changes.

rule.name: AvoidInstantiatingObjectsInLoops
rule.message: Avoid creating new objects inside loops -- consider moving the instantiation outside the loop.
rule.description:
Creating objects inside a loop may cause unnecessary garbage collection pressure when the same instance could be constructed once outside the loop and reused. If the object can be safely shared or reset across iterations, move its construction before the loop.
Kotlin equivalent of Java rule: category/java/performance.xml/AvoidInstantiatingObjectsInLoops

property.name: javaEquivalentRule
property.description: Java rule this Kotlin rule is based on; update this rule when the Java rule changes.

property.name: xpath

rule.name: BigIntegerInstantiation
rule.message: Use BigInteger.ZERO, BigInteger.ONE, or BigInteger.TEN instead of BigInteger.valueOf(0/1/10).
rule.description:
BigInteger.valueOf(0), BigInteger.valueOf(1), and BigInteger.valueOf(10) allocate unnecessarily; the JDK already provides BigInteger.ZERO, BigInteger.ONE, and BigInteger.TEN as cached constants.
Kotlin idiomatic alternatives also include the extension functions: - 0.toBigInteger(), 1.toBigInteger(), 10.toBigInteger() However, the named constants are more readable and allocation-free.
Kotlin equivalent of Java rule: category/java/performance.xml/BigIntegerInstantiation

property.name: javaEquivalentRule
property.description: Java rule this Kotlin rule is based on; update this rule when the Java rule changes.

property.name: xpath
property.value: //PostfixUnaryExpression[ pmd-kotlin:matchesSig('java.math.BigInteger#valueOf(*)') and PostfixUnarySuffix/CallSuffix/ValueArguments/ValueArgument[ pmd-kotlin:nodeText() = '0' or pmd-kotlin:nodeText() = '1' or pmd-kotlin:nodeText() = '10' or pmd-kotlin:nodeText() = '0L' or pmd-kotlin:nodeText() = '1L' or pmd-kotlin:nodeText() = '10L' ] ]

rule.name: ConsecutiveLiteralAppends
rule.message: Consecutive calls to append() with string literals should be combined into a single call
rule.description: Consecutive calls to `StringBuilder.append()` or `StringBuffer.append()` with string literals can be combined into a single `append()` call, reducing method call overhead.

property.name: xpath

rule.name: InefficientEmptyStringCheck
rule.message: String.trim().length / String.trim().isEmpty() is an inefficient way to check for a blank string; use isBlank() instead.
rule.description:
Calling `str.trim()` (or `str.strip()`) before checking `.length` or `.isEmpty()` creates an unnecessary temporary `String` object just to discard it.
Kotlin idiomatic alternatives: - `str.isBlank()` -- equivalent to `str.trim().isEmpty()`, checks Unicode whitespace - `str.isEmpty()` -- checks only for zero length, no trimming needed
Kotlin equivalent of Java rule: category/java/performance.xml/InefficientEmptyStringCheck

property.name: javaEquivalentRule
property.description: Java rule this Kotlin rule is based on; update this rule when the Java rule changes.

rule.name: InefficientStringBuffering
rule.message: Avoid concatenating non-literals in a StringBuilder.append() call; use chained append() calls instead.
rule.description:
Passing a `+` concatenation expression to `StringBuilder.append()` or `StringBuffer.append()` creates an intermediate `String` object unnecessarily. Use chained `append()` calls or Kotlin's `buildString { }` builder instead.
Kotlin idiomatic alternatives: - Chain: `sb.append("prefix").append(variable)` - Builder: `buildString { append("prefix"); append(variable) }`
Kotlin equivalent of Java rule: category/java/performance.xml/InefficientStringBuffering

property.name: javaEquivalentRule
property.description: Java rule this Kotlin rule is based on; update this rule when the Java rule changes.

rule.name: StringInstantiation
rule.message: Avoid creating String objects unnecessarily; use a String literal instead.
rule.description:
Strings are immutable in Kotlin/JVM. Creating String(someString) just copies the reference to a new object; use the string literal or variable directly.
Note: Unlike Java's `new String(str)`, Kotlin's `kotlin.String` does not expose a String(String) copy constructor, so this rule primarily catches Java-interop patterns where the Java constructor is invoked explicitly (e.g. via reflection or a helper that calls `java.lang.String(String)`).
Kotlin equivalent of Java rule: category/java/performance.xml/StringInstantiation

property.name: javaEquivalentRule
property.description: Java rule this Kotlin rule is based on; update this rule when the Java rule changes.

rule.name: StringToString
rule.message: Avoid calling toString() on a String; it returns the same object.
rule.description:
Calling toString() on a String is a no-op; the same String instance is returned. Remove the unnecessary call.
Kotlin equivalent of Java rule: category/java/performance.xml/StringToString

property.name: javaEquivalentRule
property.description: Java rule this Kotlin rule is based on; update this rule when the Java rule changes.

rule.name: TooFewBranchesForWhenExpression
rule.message: A when expression with too few branches can be replaced with a simpler if/else.
rule.description:
A condition-only `when` expression (no subject) with very few branches carries more syntax overhead than a plain `if`/`if-else`. Use `if` when only one or two conditions are needed.
Note: `when` expressions with a typed subject (e.g., `when (sealedValue)`) are always allowed regardless of branch count. They serve as exhaustive pattern matching on enums and sealed classes, which is idiomatic Kotlin even with only two outcomes.
Kotlin equivalent of Java rule: category/java/performance.xml/TooFewBranchesForASwitchStatement

property.name: javaEquivalentRule
property.description: Java rule this Kotlin rule is based on; update this rule when the Java rule changes.

property.name: minimumNumberCaseForAWhen
property.description: Minimum number of when-branches to justify using a when without a subject instead of if/else

rule.name: UseIndexOfChar
rule.message: Use indexOf(Char) instead of indexOf(String) when searching for a single character.
rule.description:
String.indexOf(String) and String.lastIndexOf(String) perform full substring search when given a single-character argument. The Char overloads -- indexOf(Char) and lastIndexOf(Char) -- use a simpler linear scan without the overhead of multi-char matching.
Kotlin equivalent of Java rule: category/java/performance.xml/UseIndexOfChar

property.name: javaEquivalentRule
property.description: Java rule this Kotlin rule is based on; update this rule when the Java rule changes.

property.name: xpath

rule.name: UselessStringValueOf
rule.message: Avoid using String.valueOf(); use a string template or toString() instead
rule.description: There is no need to call `String.valueOf()` to convert a value to a String in Kotlin. Use a string template (`"$value"`) or `value.toString()` instead, which is more idiomatic and avoids unnecessary overhead.

rule.name: UseStringBuilderForStringAppends
rule.message: Use a StringBuilder instead of concatenating strings with += inside a loop.
rule.description:
Each use of `+=` to concatenate a String inside a loop creates a hidden temporary StringBuilder, a backing char[] array, and a new String object per iteration. These temporaries must all be garbage collected. Using an explicit StringBuilder outside the loop and calling `append()` avoids these repeated allocations.
Kotlin equivalent of Java rule: category/java/performance.xml/UseStringBufferForStringAppends

property.name: javaEquivalentRule
property.description: Java rule this Kotlin rule is based on; update this rule when the Java rule changes.

property.name: xpath

rule.name: UseStringBuilderLength
rule.message: Use StringBuilder.length instead of StringBuilder.toString().length to avoid creating a temporary String.
rule.description:
Calling toString() on a StringBuilder or StringBuffer just to read its length allocates a temporary String object unnecessarily. StringBuilder and StringBuffer already expose a length property that returns the current character count directly.
Kotlin equivalent of Java rule: category/java/performance.xml/UseStringBuilderLength

property.name: javaEquivalentRule
property.description: Java rule this Kotlin rule is based on; update this rule when the Java rule changes.
